explicit_text: I was an undergraduate at Fisk University in the 1930s, and it was a hub for interracial meetings, unlike Vanderbilt. White people would come to Fisk for public affairs, concerts, and lectures without any segregation, and I was president of the Student Government in my senior year. Fisk was also involved in significant events, like when President Franklin D. Roosevelt visited in 1934 to hear the Fisk choir sing at Jubilee Hall. The campus was a center of activism and community, especially during the lynching of Cordie Cheek, which deeply affected us all.
implicit_text: I was an undergraduate at a historically black university in Nashville in the 1930s, and it was a hub for interracial meetings, unlike the nearby private university. White people would come to this institution for public affairs, concerts, and lectures without any segregation, and I was president of the Student Government in my senior year. This school was also involved in significant events, like when President Franklin D. Roosevelt visited in 1934 to hear the choir sing at its iconic hall. The campus was a center of activism and community, especially during the lynching of Cordie Cheek, which deeply affected us all.

explicit_text: During my undergraduate years in the 1930s, Vanderbilt University never hosted interracial meetings, though their students often came over to Fisk. I recall a notable incident when sociologist E. Franklin Frazier was invited to a meeting at Vanderbilt, which upset Chancellor Kirkland. I also took the Scholastic Aptitude Test at Vanderbilt in my senior year, where I felt very uncomfortable due to the racial atmosphere. Overall, Vanderbilt was a place of exclusion compared to Fisk during that time.

explicit_text: I heard that Robert E. Park, a distinguished sociologist, was involved in a meeting at Vanderbilt where E. Franklin Frazier was invited, though I’m not certain of all the details. This event happened during my undergraduate years at Fisk in the early 1930s and caused quite a stir, especially with Vanderbilt’s administration. Park’s presence symbolized some of the early academic interracial engagements, even if limited.

explicit_text: E. Franklin Frazier was a significant figure during my time at Fisk; he left at the end of my junior year in 1934 to go to Harvard. I remember he was invited to a luncheon or meeting at Vanderbilt, which upset Chancellor Kirkland. Frazier’s move to Harvard marked an important transition, and his presence was a symbol of black academic achievement during that period.

explicit_text: After my senior year at Fisk, I applied to Harvard University for graduate school in 1934. Before the GREs, I had to take the Scholastic Aptitude Test, which was administered at Vanderbilt, and that experience was quite uncomfortable due to racial attitudes. E. Franklin Frazier also went to Harvard in 1934, which was a notable move for black scholars at the time. I eventually went to Harvard and returned to teach at Fisk in 1936-37.

explicit_text: In the spring of my senior year at Fisk, I had to take the Scholastic Aptitude Test to apply for graduate school at Harvard. The test was held at Vanderbilt University, and it was my first time on that campus. I felt very uncomfortable because the professor administering the test seemed shocked to see me there and threw the test at me. I often wondered how I did on that test given the tense atmosphere.

I was familiar with Phi Beta Kappa because my sponsor, Ted Currier, was a member. That small detail stuck with me during an otherwise uncomfortable experience on the Vanderbilt campus.

explicit_text: In the fall of my senior year at Fisk in 1934, a young black man named Cordie Cheek was lynched near the campus. Although he was not a Fisk student, his death deeply disturbed the student body and sparked protests. This tragic event happened just weeks before President Roosevelt’s visit to Fisk, and I was right in the middle of the campus response as president of the Student Government.

explicit_text: In November 1934, President Franklin D. Roosevelt made a historic visit to Nashville, stopping at the Hermitage and Fisk University to hear the Fisk choir sing. As president of the Student Government, I worked closely with the Secret Service to prepare for his visit, including managing student behavior around Jubilee Hall. His visit was controversial among Nashville citizens, some of whom were upset by the lack of segregation at Fisk during the event.

explicit_text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt was traveling to Warm Springs, Georgia in 1934, and on his way, he stopped in Nashville to visit the Hermitage and Fisk University. This stop was part of his well-known retreat to Warm Springs for health reasons. The visit to Nashville was significant because it included engaging with the black community at Fisk, which was unusual at the time.

explicit_text: Jubilee Hall at Fisk University was the site where President Roosevelt and Mrs. Roosevelt heard the Fisk choir sing during their 1934 visit. The choir performed on the steps of Jubilee Hall, and bleachers were set up for citizens to watch the President’s arrival. As Student Government president, I helped enforce rules, such as no students being allowed upstairs to look out the windows during the visit.

explicit_text: During President Roosevelt’s 1934 visit to Fisk, a white man told me he had voted the Democratic ticket all his life but would never vote for the Democrats again if the President came to Fisk and allowed no special seating for white people. This conversation revealed the racial tensions and political implications of Roosevelt’s engagement with black communities. It was a striking moment that showed how race and politics were deeply intertwined in that era.

explicit_text: Tom Jones was the president of Fisk University when the YMCA graduate school closed, and he bought much of their equipment, including outdated furniture. This happened sometime after my graduation, but I remember the impact it had on Fisk’s resources. Jones was instrumental in maintaining Fisk’s academic environment during challenging times.

explicit_text: I taught in St. Augustine, Florida starting in 1939 and stayed there for about four years. This period coincided with the Southern Conference for Human Welfare meeting in Birmingham in 1938, which I was aware of and later joined. My time in St. Augustine was part of my broader involvement in civil rights and interracial activism.

Durham was also the site of a 1942 meeting where a group of black leaders planned what became known as the Durham Statement.

explicit_text: I joined the Southern Conference for Human Welfare sometime around the late 1930s or early 1940s. The group held meetings in Birmingham, Chattanooga, Nashville, and even in Durham at the Washington Duke Hotel. These meetings were focused on egalitarian ideals, though we faced discrimination, such as being refused service in the hotel dining room, which we protested by bringing in outside food to eat together.

explicit_text: The Washington Duke Hotel in downtown Durham was the site of a Southern Conference for Human Welfare meeting during the early 1940s. Although we were allowed to meet there, the hotel refused to serve black attendees in the dining room, which led to protests where we brought in outside food to eat together. This incident highlighted the persistent segregation and discrimination even in supposedly progressive settings.
